exemplar.title: How can a 100ml perfume bottle be optimized to minimize costs?

detailedExemplarData.title: How can a 100ml perfume bottle be optimized to minimize costs?

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear logical sequence with introduction, body and conclusion
Effective headings and sub-headings that guide the reader
Conclusion ties together optimized dimensions coherently
weaknesses: Peripheral historical background and repeated definitions reduce conciseness
Interrupted flow by long blocks of GeoGebra code
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments show solid coherence and organization (Band 3) but conciseness suffers (Band 2). Overall, the work sits âmostly relevant with some unnecessary elementsâ and âadequately organizedâ, meriting a midâband score of 2/4.

strengths: Correct use of key symbols (Ï, r_opt, SA) and function notation
Multiple representations (diagrams, tables, GeoGebra models) support understanding
Logical chain of calculation with clear introductions to major steps
weaknesses: Inconsistent unit notation and occasional boldface variables in text
Empty notation table and some low-resolution images
Long code listings and occasional algebraic slips hamper clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands are rated at Band 3, indicating âsome appropriate useâ of language, representations and structure but with lapses. The minor inconsistencies and interruptions justify a score of 2/4 for âsomeâ rather than âmostly or highly consistentâ mathematical communication.

strengths: Clear personal motivation linked to perfumery and cost reduction
Evidence of independent thought in selecting a real-world optimization problem
weaknesses: Modeling choices follow standard procedures with limited innovation
Some GeoGebra steps copied directly from help menus
Creativity is good but not exceptional
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strands are at Band 2 (significant independent thinking and good creativity) but overall the approach remains fairly conventional. To reflect modest but genuine engagement within the 9-point total, a score of 1/3 is appropriate.

strengths: Some acknowledgement of sources of inaccuracy and practical implications
Numerical comparison of outcomes (surface area difference)
weaknesses: Reflection remains at a surface level with limited depth
Basic analysis without rigorous error quantification
No comprehensive evaluation or future implications
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All reflection strands are Band 1, indicating only âlimitedâ reflection and âbasicâ analysis. This justifies the minimal score of 1/3.

strengths: Relevant calculus techniques and solids of revolution used appropriately
Correct execution of critical-point calculation and disk-method integration
Strong procedural accuracy in deriving optimized surface area
weaknesses: Minor errors in unit conversion and discrepancies between table and code output
Polynomial mis-fit in surface-area integral not fully reconciled
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Use of Mathematics strands are assessed at two Band 4s and one Band 3. While application is largely appropriate and at course level, noticeable minor errors preclude the top band. A score of 3/6 reflects a good but not flawless use of mathematics.

strandSelections.reasoning: Each major calculation is introduced, worked through and briefly interpreted, giving a generally clear logical chain.  Occasional algebraic slips (unit conversion, SA simplification typos) and long code listings hamper clarity, but overall structure and explanation remain good.

The student shows good understanding of optimisation and volume/area formulae, and most algebraic manipulation is correct.  However, conversion from âcubic unitsâ to cmÂ³ is flawed, and the surface-area integral suffers from possible polynomial mis-fits, indicating minor but noticeable errors that preclude the highest band.

Diagrams of the three candidate solids, GeoGebra screenshots, polynomial equations, tables and final comparative percentages give multiple representations that support understanding.  Some images are low-resolution and one table is empty, so the use is âeffectiveâ rather than âhighly effectiveâ.

The exploration proceeds in a logical sequence: introduction, justification of shape, algebraic optimisation, GeoGebra modelling, integration and conclusion.  Each mathematical section builds on the previous one, so the reader can follow the argument without major jumps.  Nevertheless, occasional digressions (lengthy historical background, repeated explanations of basic formulae) weaken the overall flow, preventing the work from reaching the very highest level of coherence.

Using GeoGebra point tracing and polynomial fitting to create a 3-D solid, then integrating for volume/area, demonstrates creativity and initiative beyond routine calculus.  Yet the technique follows well-known CAS procedures and some parts are copied verbatim from help menus, so the level is âgoodâ rather than âexceptionalâ.

The student recognises that the calculated volume differs from 100 cmÂ³ and that unit conversion may be flawed, but the analysis of these errors is basic and no rigorous recalculation is attempted.  This satisfies only the descriptor for âbasic analysisâ.

Outcomes are compared numerically (24 % larger surface area) and the student asserts that the optimised design would lower cost, yet there is no quantitative cost model or exploration of practical feasibility.  Evaluation therefore remains limited.

Reflection is present but limited: the student notes sources of inaccuracy (image distortion, point selection) and suggests a flatter-bottomed sphere for future work.  Discussion stops at surface comments and does not probe mathematical implications or alternative models in depth.

The student employs correct symbols (Ï, r_opt, SA), uses function notation and derivatives appropriately, and labels integrals clearly.  Minor lapses, such as inconsistent unit notation and the occasional use of boldface variables in prose, keep the language just below full consistency.

The work clearly reflects the studentâs personal interest in perfumery and cost reduction, and the decision to compare an existing bottle with an optimised design shows a personal route through the mathematics.  The approach, while personal, is not especially innovative, placing it in the middle band.

Much of the text is directly relevant to cost-minimising bottle design, yet substantial peripheral material (historical anecdotes, market statistics, step-by-step screenshots) adds length without advancing the mathematics.  Repetition of definitions (e.g., solid of revolution) further reduces conciseness, situating the work in the âmostly relevant with some unnecessary elementsâ band.

Optimisation with calculus, solids of revolution, definite integration and unit conversion are all relevant to the problem and used in a largely appropriate manner.  Mathematics is applied throughout the exploration and not merely stated, so effectiveness is evident.

Selecting a real-world cost problem, choosing a composite shape, and devising an optimisation model show noticeable independent thought beyond textbook examples.  However, the modelling choices (e.g. ixed bottle thickness, simplified mouth) are fairly standard and not deeply original, so the evidence is âsignificantâ rather than âoutstandingâ.

Headings, sub-headings and numbered sections give the piece a clear structure, and tables/figures are embedded where referenced.  Introduction, main body and conclusion are evident.  However, some tables are unfinished, and long blocks of GeoGebra code interrupt readability, so the organisation, while good, is short of the exemplary standard.

Calculus techniques (first-derivative test, arc length integrals) are solidly at HL level and used correctly in most instances.  While some computational shortcuts appear, the mathematical level is fully commensurate with the course and generally well applied.

annotations.comment: The extensive historical background provides interesting context but is peripheral to the mathematical aim. Condense this section to enhance conciseness and maintain focus on optimization.
The research question is stated clearly and concisely, providing a focused mathematical problem that guides the exploration effectively.
Consider adding a brief outline of the planned methodology or structure on the title page to provide immediate clarity on the approach for the reader.
Justification for choosing soda-lime glass is well explained, showing consideration of both cost and sustainability factors relevant to a commercial product.
Figure 1.1 caption omits scale and dimension details. Include all labelled measurements and units in the caption to ensure the image stands alone.
The notation table header is present but all cells are blank. Populate this table with symbol definitions to support consistent communication of variables.
Ensure consistent notation and alignment in the variable table, for example by standardizing font size and style for subscripts like $$SA_{opt}$$ and $$r_{opt}$$.
The transition into the âJustification of perfume bottle shapeâ section is logical and maintains coherence in the explorationâs structure.
The personal motivation for selecting this problem is strong and clearly articulated, demonstrating genuine engagement with the context of perfumery and its importance.
The inline math tag around the citation year appears as math rather than a reference. Use consistent text formatting for years to avoid confusion with mathematical notation.
The assumption that material cost is directly proportional to surface area is reasonable but would be strengthened by brief justification or citation from manufacturing cost data.
The solution of $$f'(r)=0$$ to obtain $$r_{opt}=\sqrt[3]{\frac{60}{\pi}}$$ is executed correctly, showcasing solid understanding of critical-point calculations.
Including explicit sign analysis of $$f'(r)$$ for values below and above $$r_{opt}$$ would fully validate the extremum and strengthen the logical argument.
The diagram explaining shape justification is low resolution and hard to interpret. Replace it with a sharper image or schematic for improved clarity.
The derivation of the sphereâs radius and surface area is correctly notated and clearly presented, demonstrating strong understanding of basic geometrical formulas.
In the cylinder section, explanatory text is separated awkwardly from the equations. Reorganize into sequential, labeled calculation steps to improve readability.
The composite hemisphere-on-cylinder calculations apply both calculus and algebra correctly, illustrating effective use of relevant mathematics for a non-standard shape.
When simplifying to $$h = \frac{100}{\pi r^2} - \frac{2}{3}r$$, include a concise explanation of combining terms to guide readers through the algebraic manipulation.
The algebraic step combining $$3\pi r^2 + \frac{200}{r} - \frac{4\pi r^2}{3}$$ into a single expression lacks intermediate detail. Show the common-denominator step to improve clarity.
Defining $$f(r)=\frac{5}{3}\pi r^2 + 200r^{-1}$$ is precise and prepares for differentiation, demonstrating effective use of HL-level optimization techniques.
Before substituting numeric values into the height formula, further symbolic simplification could reduce complexity and minimize potential arithmetic errors in later steps.
The calculation of optimized surface area $$111.98\,\text{cm}^2$$ is well-presented with correct unit notation, reflecting strong procedural accuracy.
The practical commentary on travel size is useful but could be deepened by estimating cost savings from reduced surface area or by discussing real-world manufacturing constraints.
Long procedural GeoGebra instructions interrupt the mathematical narrative. Consider relocating detailed software steps to an appendix to maintain flow.
The parametric surface representation using a matrix of trigonometric functions is an effective use of multiple representations to model a solid of revolution.
Manual smoothing of the polynomial in Section III is acknowledged but the resulting impact on volume and surface-area calculations is not quantified. Include an error analysis.
Clearly stating $$\displaystyle V=\int_a^b\pi y^2\,dx$$ highlights correct application of the disk method, illustrating solid understanding of volume by revolution.
The extensive integral for surface area would be clearer if broken into numbered sub-integrals with labels for each section to guide the reader through the computation.
There is a discrepancy between the surface area in metric units (120.10 cmÂ²) in the table and 139.18 cmÂ² in the code output. Reconcile these values and correct the conversion.
The conclusion effectively summarizes optimized dimensions and comparative results, tying together the findings coherently and logically.
The evaluation identifies practical limitations but remains at a surface level. Recommend deeper critical analysis of assumptions and consideration of alternative bottle shapes.
